author: @barnowl6807
text: You are misleading people.  First, a solar voltaic cell  acts as a current source, not a voltage source.  Having only a voltage reading is not an indication of the capability of the cell.  You must also measure current.   You are using a multicell panel.  The "additional" light is simply spreading out under your coverings and exciting more cells.  That is the only way you will get more voltage.  The panel has a maximum output over a relatively narrow spectrum of light.  The fluorescent color should be chosen for maximum compatibility with that spectrum or it will only add heat.

author: @ericthecyclist
text:
I don't think that claims of improvement are supported by the demonstration.
1) In order to believe the light guide had any value, I'd need to see the effect on voltage of having the light coming in at a variety of angles. My expectation is that light guide would degrade performance as the light got closer to perpendicular. The question is, how much.
2) Is the flourescent material is actually making a difference or, or would a sheet of white paper or aluminum foil worked as well?
3) How do you scale light guide and flourescent material trick to a set of panels, rather than just a slice of one.

author: @Mavrik9000
text:
Interesting, but the light was barely shining on it as most of that light was coming from an incidence angle that may be causing the majority of it to reflect upwards and away from the cells. That is of course until much of it was directed towards the cell through the LCD light guide (probably acrylic) and by the jacket's reflective coating back through the LCD light guide. 
In other words, this would only be a valid test if the light beam from the handheld light was directed from the proper angle in the first place, and then compare the output to that obtained with the addition of the other components.
Also, the overhead ceiling lighting and all the reflective surfaces in the room need to be accounted for and isolated or removed.
The setup here is showing about a 50% increase, but I suspect it would be between 1% to 2% if tested properly.

author: @zachcrawford5
text: Now you need a dichroic coating on the two larger surfaces of your light guide that is reflective to the wavelengths that your fluorescent molecules emit at. So light can enter you panel easily enough but then when inside is converted by the fluorescent molecules into a wavelength that is trapped due to the dichroic coating, this will prevent light "leaking out" the back or front of the guide and force most of that light to the panels at the edges.

author: @beyondfossil
text:
Interesting experiment.  But I think most of the solar cell improvement seen there is just the increased light flux from the reflected light off the safety vest that would normally otherwise travel straight through.  A second test sample would be a simple white non-florescent cloth and compare it against the florescent vest.

The electron "band gap" of the semiconductor's doping elements determines the solar cell's photon sensitivity.  Boron is used for P-type and Phosphorous for the N-type.   This makes current commercial single-junction solar cells sensitive to red and infrared photons only.  Most of the blue photons just bounce off and do not kick a valence electron out of its orbit to induce an electric field.

What's going to work better than florescent pigments for solar cells are transparent "Quantum Dot" (QD) films that can be laid right on top of the solar cell in its own layer under the glass.  Kind of like florescent pigments, QD films will convert shortwave "bluish" light into longer wavelength light -- specifically infrared light so solar QD films won't look brighter to human eyes but will have a dramatic effect on solar cells.  The articles I read are that a simple QD film layer can increase current solar cell efficiency some 50%.  This will especially help on cloudy days where there's mostly just scattered blue light around from the clouds.

I believe the biggest hurdle right now is the Quantum Dot films are made of organic compounds so have longevity issues in the blazing heat and UV conditions a typical solar panel experiences.

QD films are not theoretical and are already being used in latest commercial LED monitors to improve power efficiency and light purity because the LED light source has a slight blue tint that is otherwise just filtered out with a yellow film.

author: @nebojsatodorovic9010

The florescent material was Strontium Aluminate from the company mule light. I tried to make a deal with them to help me get a solar panel made but they didn't much care about it or other companies for that matter. My goal was to make a panel that worked at night. Working better in the day was just a side effect.

while i like the concept would it not be much harder to apply in real world scenario? the only reason i ask is because in this model we are measuring a single cell, but panels aren't made up of single cells, they have multiple, so would having stacks of acrylic with flourescent materials not then start reducing light transmission to the other cells and reduce their overall output of said panel would it not? I realise there are varying factors to this issue that add complexity, time of day, angle in relation to the sun, possibly would make panel cleaning harder too.

It just seems that a 30% increase in voltage seems like way too good an improvement to be realistic in real world terms, there has to be a compromise made somewhere...you know you don't usually get something for nothing, its kinda steering away from thermodynamics and close to the fringe like claimed free energy devices...would love to see different models on whole panels tested and the efficiency increase measured.

author: @odonald6087
text: fluorescents increase the wavelength, not reduce it, so blue gets turned into red (there are frequency doubling crystals, but they are different).  But this is fine because silicon solar actually work down to near infrared and produce one electron per photon no matter what the frequency is.  You can put the fluorescent on the front face if it is applied carefully because the long wavelengths will pass through the material, and the shorter wavelengths will create fluorescence.  In practice this isn't used because none of the readily available fluorescents are sufficiently efficient to average more than one infrared photo per incoming photon, and the emitted photons go in all directions, so they end up making the cell less efficient.  silicon cells catch any photon less than 1150nm or so, but if you want to make two photons you need at least half that wavelength, so 575nm, but there is only about 30% more energy available this way.

author: @TimLongson
text: Have you heard of Cambridge Photon Technology (CPT), it's a company developing tech (already exists within labs) which could increase the existing efficiency rate by over 50% more than it is today & beyond, by placing a layer above existing solar panels, & it converts up to a potential of the remaining 70% of light into a form that solar panels can also make use of. Silicon can only convert light at the red end of the spectrum, with much of the green and blue photons going to waste. A polymer semiconductor film can absorb these green & blue photons, and light emitting clumps of material called quantum dots convert them into a more useful wavelength. To put it another way, a blue or green photo excites a quantum dot, which then emits 2 near-infrared photons which the silicon solar panel below can make use of, along with the normal longer wavelength light passing through the film, and is absorbed by silicon in the usual way. Net effect: many more usable photons hit the silicon solar panel & so much more power is output from the same light!

author: @pikotech1
text:
Great video Rob. Interesting addition to this is the idea of glow in the dark paint (I have mountains of it), being used as an effective solar battery.

Run your cells as normal during the day, but also charge up an equivalent sized board painted with glow paint during the day too. At night (and could be automated I guess), pull the glowing board on top of your solar cells facing them. Night solar in battery form!

Edit: Rob, if you want a bunch of free glow powder, give me a shout, happy to post some out to you!

author: @zer0nix
text:
I'm confused about the claim that the light guide increases efficiency by 50 percent. I haven't been able to find exactly how they quantify this but I'm curious if they mean that the light guide increases efficiency according to the size of the entire panel including the light guide or just the efficiency of the photovoltaic elements. If it is the former, which I find unlikely since so much light passes through the light guides, that is super exciting because that means this is a more space efficient design than our existing flat panels! If it is the latter, it's still exciting but you would have to weigh the cost of the improved photovoltaics vs a larger area of more ordinary cells. Of course, like you say, I can't wait to see how an actually well designed unit does!

Also, I'm kind of surprised you didn't get some clear fluorescent sheets (typically used for 'cool' school accessories like folders or clipboards) and lay them on top of a solar panel to see if the amount of captured uv offsets the luminosity lost by putting something in front of the panel.

author: @jamesross1003
text: I have actually done this as an experiment years ago when I was in elementary school.  I am 51 years old now.  You do the math.  This was back when solar cells were just getting very popular on devices like calculators.  I took the cells off of the calculators(if I remember correctly I used the cells off of 4 of them).  I had an old chemistry set that had a small amount of radium(it was a set from the late 40's I believe).  It was the set that had experiments involving glow in the dark things, a cloud chamber and others using radioactive compounds.  It had an experiment that showed you how to make a glow in the dark pigment from radium.  Much like the paint on old watch faces.  I took that and lined the face of the cells with it.  It gave about a 25 percent increase in output and also continued to produce small amounts of current after light was removed from the cells.  It works.  Great video Robert!

author: @tonysummers2334
text: Could the back of a solar panel create the 6th side of a water tank? So the water passing through the tank cools the panel improving its efficiency. Also the water will heat up giving us hot water.

replies.author: @taylorwestmore4664
replies.text: Absolutely. Tech ingredients have a couple videos that touch on this subject. In "Get 4X The Power From Your Solar Panels!" they use mirrors to concentrate light onto a single panel, and while this boosts the power output and quantum efficiency, the temperature degrades the cell efficiency severely by increasing internal resistance and damaging the PN junctions. In another video "Should you really use solar panels?", they do a side by side comparison of solar photovoltaic panels vs solar thermal water heating panels, and determined that the PV panel collects only a fraction, between 1/3rd and 1/6th or less of the available heat energy of the Sun due to the 20% or so efficiency converting light to electricity. The big takeaway is that a combination of concentrated solar and thermal panels should both maximize the efficiency and lifetime of the solar panel, minimize the cost, and utilize the otherwise wasted heat energy to heat your water.

replies.text: I am with Taylor on this one mate - absolutely

author: @XtomJamesExtra
text:
A couple of problems; one the shirt isn't fluorescent, merely reflective to make them more visible to drivers, especially at night with headlights; and two, all you did is reflect more light into the solar cell. You didn't use fluorescence as you initially described. 

Nonetheless, the initial idea and secondary effect shown, albeit unrelated, are valid points. Use of reflective surfaces to direct more light into a solar panel does improve efficiency and power production, and the use of fluorescence could be used to store light which is later released at night to provide at least a trickle of power production by solar panels. 

However, arguably, if you want to improve solar panels, the simplest method is to just have it capture more light. Take a solar panel that is translucent, set it on a frame, and the backing of the frame should be painted the whitest paint one can make. 97 %+ reflectivity of light. Any light that isn't captured that passes through the solar panel is reflected back at it allowing for a secondary capture. Doing this should improved efficiency by around 40-50%. If you add fluorescent materials onto this backing, that material will release light at night allowing the panel to produce power for longer without direct light from the sun.

replies.author: @Earzone63
replies.text:
Why is high vis clothing fluorescent and reflective?
The sun's ultraviolet rays react with the fluorescent colours to make them appear to 'glow' increasing daytime visibility.

fluorescent material differs from retroreflective materials in that it absorbs some invisible ultraviolet light from sunlight, then special pigments release those UV light rays as more visible light. It can only do this in areas where there is natural light.

In the demonstration there was the same amount of incident light, therefore the higher voltage meant more power / greater efficiency.
In fact i've measured the efficiency (also called shockley queisser limit) for a type of solar cell called DSSC that worked with incident fluorescent light - and by the strict definition we exceeded the ~30% limit which is the case for silicon cells under sun spectrum -- the trouble with DSSCs however is the total power output / the absolute area under the curve.

replies.author: @XtomJamesExtra
replies.text:
@Earzone63 Fluorescence and the Appearance of fluorescence are not the same thing. The very section you quoted notes that difference. "The sun's ultraviolet rays react with the fluorescent colours to make them appear to 'glow' increasing daytime visibility."

They "appear" to glow, they're not glowing however. 

There was not the same amount of incident light. The flashlight was not directed at the solar cell. The LCD panel refractor directed some of that light to the solar cell, the safety gear reflected more. That's it. More importantly, that flashlight Doesn't Emit UV light. It is not a full spectrum light source. So even if the material is UV reactive causing a fluorescing effect, it wouldn't be produced by that flashlight. 

All that was demonstrated was that if a material reflects more light into the solar cell, it produces more electrical current.

replies.author: @XtomJamesExtra
replies.text: As for the absolute limit of efficiency, this applies only to the direct light and initial capture. It has to do with the electron exchange caused by photons hitting the material the solar cell is made from. There is a limit to the amount of electron bumping that can occur. The back side of the cells are just as reactive as the front side, but rarely ever used due to most solar panels being oriented to receive light from only one direction. In a translucent cell, without a backing, excess light allowed to pass through the cell can still be reflected back at the cell to produce more power. This is the principle behind the transparent solar panels developed in 2019 that could become a replacement for windows in large buildings and homes. A conventional solar panel could be made just as easily in this same method, while using a high incident reflective surface to bring more light back to the solar panel. Furthermore, the whitest paint in the world, currently, using titanium oxide, converts UV light to a visible white spectrum upon reflection. It reflects 99.97% of all light (and I mean all light; radio spectrum all the way up to gamma, and was initially designed for space-craft use). Higher energy light is converted to a visible range or near visible range which solar panels can use.

replies.author: @Earzone63
replies.text:
@XtomJamesExtra lol if it's not glowing but just appearing to glow then what is the difference? what would you say a fluorescent material is doing - i could be wrong but i believe it is transitioning photons/electrons between HOMO LUMO molecular orbitals -- this is how incident uv wavelengths can be translated into more green wavelengths (1.1eV) suitable for silicon solar PhotoVoltaics. 
Funny you mention TiO2 because that was the substrate we used to make the DSSCs, we added a nano dyes to that. 

As for Hi Vis jacket the fluorescent material has a base cloth and adhesives to secure micro glass beads that produce highly reflective bands. ...  and before you say this is just reflection I have to say idk how the uv would induce fluorescence .. but I can guess: A rain drop can split light incident at 42º into colors of different directions if the light reflects 3 times within the drop (or 4 times for a double rainbow). If two waves in the uv band are incident at a similar time (the electron's excitation 'window'), then it could access some doubly excited state that is statistically more favourable (a shorter excitation time) to make a transition of 1 'jump' at longer wavelengths instead of the 2... and there would have to be 4 photons released instead of the 2 incident ones to conserve energy -- which would explain the glow / increase in intensity.
Thanks for  prompting me to think about that some more , i hope my intuitive explanation helps !

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Yes, this is a really excellent idea. In honesty, I have been mulling over something similar for just a few weeks now, except not as a light pipe but simply as a "coating". (I guess I need to buy some tin foil! heheh). (been looking at all sorts of options but hadn't really looked at fluorescent Cloth! But honestly, super glad you are focusing on this because I wasn't sure how this would turn out. Actually I am pleasantly surprised by the boost in voltage simply by nature of the fact that the light first past into passed the acrylic, then out the other side, absorbed by the fluorescent material, then re-emitted as a new wavelength back into the acrylic to be absorbed... very very interesting. Now that, I had not anticipated to do much but clearly it is pretty decent.
What happens if you put 1 layer of the shirt (fluorescent dye) between the light and the acrylic?

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: You could use fluorescent card , paper or plastic sheets. You could use different colours to see what the optimal colour is for fluorescing light back on to any given type or make of solar cell. This would allow solar cells that cannot hardness UV light to do so by changing the frequencies of the incoming UV light to one's it can. You could do the same with different colour glow in the dark sheets too.

author: @kevinleebailey
text: This makes me think about the nuclear battery you made. Using the technique you mention but using radioactive decay to make phosphesance or fluorescence instead of light visible or none visible. You could use a combination of both.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea mate

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: Basically what I'm trying to say is a glow in the dark material charged by radioactive decay powering a solar cell. If it could be charged by other frequencies from the EMS even better. Cosmic rays , infrared , ultraviolet............ect.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering uranium glass or the radium paint that was used on pilots watches comes to mind.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Cerenkov's radiation (light) could be utilised some way too.

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: Awesome! 🎉

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thank you! Cheers!

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Mmmm, isn't Europium sometimes a beta emitter?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well europium is the resultant of beta decay of samarium 151 - but apparently some europium compound are phosphorescent  - but phosphorescent and fluorescent are different things I think

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I went poking around for europium as a result of your beta-emitter experiment.  I think it is both.  Internet description and pictures of europium fluorescing under uv, as well as used in phosphorescent dyes and paints.  Started me wondering if phosphorescence might help a PV cell on intermittent cloudy days, but then you showed this.  As far as beta emitter,  I think it is only one of the isotopes -- your choice of americium was better for your demo.

author: @unclereefer37
text: Love to see the idea but in this case, isn't the light guide really just increasing the surface area of the cell? and the shirt acting as a reflective surface increasing the effective surface area? If you used a  white piece of paper it would do the same thing as the shirt?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - I tried but I try to keep the vids to the point - plus this is backed up by an awful lot of research showing it works - it is more of a home version you can do yourself

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thanks for the reply, please keep up with the fantastic content
